content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 28, 2000


G. Paul Newkirk
22447 Rd. 203
Lindsay, California 93247

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-00-057

Dear Mr. Newkirk:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your question is general in nature and does not pertain to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for general assistance.²

QUESTION

May the Lindsay Unified School District continue to purchase items from the nursery where your wife is employed?

CONCLUSION

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not apply to governmental agencies, but apply to a public official who is making, participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision. As a board member, you will be disqualified from

___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear in Title 2, Sections 18109 - 18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

² General assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided in an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(e).) Further, please note that the Commission is currently considering amendments to the conflict-of-interest regulations which may affect the analysis contained in this letter. You can monitor the progress of these changes at the Commission's Website (www.fppc.ca.gov).

F:\DATA\LEGAL\ADVICE LTRS\00057.WPD

Our File No. I-00-057
Page 2

taking part in decisions related to the acquisition of goods and services from the nursery for
which your wife works if your wife receives $500 or more in salary from the nursery in a
calendar year. You may also be disqualified from taking part in decisions which are not directly
related to the provision of goods and services from the nursery, but which are substantially likely
to give rise to a need for goods or services from the nursery. Additionally, you will need to
contact the Attorney General's Office with respect to other provisions of law such as Government
Code Section 1090.

FACTS

You are an elected official of the Lindsay Unified School District. As of February 15,
1999, your wife began working part-time (20 hours per week) for a nursery in your district as a
floral designer. The district has an account at the nursery, and occasionally makes purchases
there for non-bid items. Items to be purchased would include grass seed, bedding plants, flower
arrangements and other miscellaneous items.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a financial interest in a decision if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family,
or one or more of several economic interests. (Section 87103.) The Commission uses a
standard, eight-step analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of
interest in a given governmental decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Step 1: Are you a public official?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) As a member of the Lindsay Unified School District
board of trustees, you are a public official under Sections 82048 and 82041.

Step 2: Are the school board's transactions with the nursery where your wife is
employed governmental decisions covered by the Act?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official "make[s],
participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest."
(Section 87100; Regulation 18700(b)(2).) Any actions taken by the board of trustees itself with
regard to decisions to contract with, pay invoices from or otherwise do business with your wife's
employer are governmental decisions under the Act. Other decisions, such as a vote to re-sod a

\\MAINSRV\VOL1\DATA\LEGAL\ADVICE LTRS\00057.WPD

Our File No. I-00-057
Page 3

football field or build a new school that will require landscaping services, are also governmental
decisions. (Regulation 18702.1.) Thus, the Act's conflicts rules apply to these decisions.

Step 3: What are your economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from
economic interests. The "economic interests" from which conflicts of interest may arise are
described by Section 87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5. There are six kinds:

•    A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
     direct or indirect investment of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation
     18703.1(a));
•    A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she is a
     director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management
     (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b));
•    A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct
     or indirect interest of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);
•    An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
     totaling $250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c);
     Regulation 18703.3);
•    A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
     total $300 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); Regulation
     18703.4);
•    A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets,
     or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family - this is known as the
     "personal financial effects" rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).

     Additionally, a public official's "income" for conflict-of-interest purposes includes his or
her community property interest in the income of his or her spouse. (Section 82030(a).) As a
result, and under the facts you have provided, you have two relevant economic interests. First,
you have an economic interest in the nursery that employs your wife as a source of income.
Second, you have an interest in your personal household income under the personal financial
effects rule. Since the only apparent impact of the decision on your personal finances would
derive from your economic interest in the nursery as a source of income, your economic interest
in your personal finances is not considered further.

Step 4: Is your economic interest directly or indirectly involved in decisions related
to the nursery?
     The fourth step in analyzing a potential conflict of interest is to determine whether each
of the public official's economic interests is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental

\\MAINSRV\VOL1\DATA\LEGAL\ADVICE LTRS\00057.WPD

Our File No. I-00-057
Page 4

decision at issue. (Regulation 18700(b)(4).) This step is important because it helps determine
(in the fifth step) which materiality test to use in deciding whether it is reasonably foreseeable
that the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on the economic interests.

Under the applicable regulation, an economic interest arising from a business entity
which is a source of income to a public official is directly involved in a decision when it:

• Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request (Regulation 18704.1(a)(1)); or

• Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official's agency. A source of income is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity.
(Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).)

According to the facts you provide, the school district will, from time to time, contract
with the nursery that employs your wife for certain goods and services.³ The nursery will be
directly involved in decisions by the school board to contract with, order goods or services or pay
invoices from the nursery. In other situations, where the board is voting on matters that will
foreseeably give rise to the need for goods or services from the nursery, such as a decision to
build a new school or re-sod a football field, the nursery will be an indirectly involved economic
interest.

Step 5: Choosing the materiality standards to use to decide if there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect.

Having established that the nursery will be directly involved in some of the school
board's decisions and indirectly involved in others, the next step is picking the appropriate
materiality standard for evaluating the financial effect of each of those types of decisions on your
economic interest in the nursery. (See Regulation 18700(b)(5).)

For those decisions in which the nursery is directly involved, the applicable materiality
standard is found in Regulation 18705.3(a). This regulation provides that any reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on the nursery is deemed material. A material financial effect will
give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest, unless it is established that the decision will affect
a significant segment of the public generally in substantially the same manner (Section 87103
and Regulation 18700(a)--see Step 7 analysis) or unless your participation is legally required
(Ibid, see Step 8 analysis; Section 87101; Regulation 18708).

For those decisions in which the nursery is indirectly involved, the applicable materiality

³ Government Code Section 1090 provides that an officer or employee may not make a contract in which
he or she is financially interested. This provision is outside the Political Reform Act and is not, therefore, enforced
by the Fair Political Practices Commission. Please contact the Public Inquiry Unit of the Attorney General's Office,
P.O. Box 944255, Sacramento, CA 94244-2550 or by telephone at (800) 952-5225.

\\MAINSRV1\VOL1\DATA\LEGAL\ADVICE LTRS\00057.WPD

Our File No. I-00-057
Page 5

standard is found in Regulation 18705.1(b)(7). Under this regulation, a decision will have a
material financial effect on the nursery if it will result in:
(A) an increase or decrease in gross revenues of $10,000 or more in a fiscal year;
(B) incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses of $2,500 or more in a fiscal year; or
(C) an increase or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more.

Step 6: Using the materiality standards to decide if it is reasonably foreseeable that
there will be material financial effect on the nursery as a result of the kinds of decisions the
school board is likely to be asked to make.
In a decision to select a nursery to provide goods or services to the school district, it is
reasonably foreseeable that the nursery for which your wife works will be affected financially.
This is true whether or not the decision is to contract with the nursery. If the board chooses to
contract with your wife's employer, the nursery will receive income. If the board chooses to
contract with another nursery, your wife's employer will lose the district as a source of income.
Decisions to pay invoices and order goods and services from the nursery will have similar
financial effects. Therefore, you will be disqualified from participating in these decisions unless
either the financial effect of the decision can be said to substantially similar to its effect on the
public generally (see Step 7 analysis) or your participation in a decision is legally required (see
Step 8 analysis).

Similarly, you will be disqualified from participating in any decision that will give rise to
a reasonably foreseeable need for goods or services that the nursery will provide if the decision
will have a material affect on the nursery. This is based on the fact that the school district has a
long-standing business relationship with the nursery and the fact that it is the only local provider
of these kinds of goods and services. By way of example, a decision to build a new school,
landscape or re-landscape an existing school, or re-sod a football field will be material if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will result in an increase or decrease in the nursery's
gross revenues of $10,000 or more in a fiscal year. (Regulation 18705.1(b)(7)(A).)

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. The long-standing
definition of "foreseeability" is set forth in In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198 . An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty
is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not considered to be
reasonably foreseeable. Reasonable foreseeability is determined at the time a governmental
decision is made.

Step 7: Determining whether the financial effect of the decision on your economic
interest in the nursery is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.
Under Section 87103, even if a governmental decision is reasonably foreseeable to have a
material financial effect on a public official's economic interest, there is no conflict of interest if

\\MAINSRV\VOL1\DATA\LEGALADVICE LTRS\00057.WFD

jurisdiction in substantially the same manner as it affects the economic interest. (See generally,
Regulation 18707 et seq.)
Decisions about purchasing goods or services or paying invoices for goods and services
already received from your wife's nursery will have a unique financial effect on the nursery.
Therefore, the public generally exception does not apply and you will have a disqualifying conflict
of interest in these decisions.

Step 8: Legally required participation.
In limited circumstances, even where a decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on the nurserv for which your wife works, you may be allowed to participate in the
decision if your participation is legally required. (Section 87101 and Regulation 18708.) This
provision is generally invoked when disqualification would leave a public body short of a quorum.
You provide no facts to suggest this provision is applicable.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Mark Krausse
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
